title: Inspiration & Travel Guides
subtitle: Expert insights to help you plan the perfect India journey

articles.title: Best Time to Visit India — Month by Month Guide
articles.excerpt: India is a year-round destination, but the experience varies dramatically by season. Here is everything you need to know to choose the perfect time for your trip.

sections.heading: October to March — The Golden Season
sections.body: This is the most popular time to visit India, and for good reason. The weather across most of the country is cool, dry, and incredibly pleasant. Rajasthan's deserts are comfortable for camel safaris, Kerala's backwaters glisten in golden light, and the Golden Triangle — Delhi, Agra, Jaipur — is at its most vibrant. December and January are peak months, so book early.

sections.heading: April to June — Summer Heat and Hill Stations
sections.body: The plains of India heat up considerably, but this is the perfect time to head to the Himalayas. Ladakh opens its roads in May, offering dramatic high-altitude landscapes. Himachal Pradesh and Uttarakhand are lush and cool. Kerala's monsoon arrives in June, transforming the state into a carpet of deep green — ideal for Ayurveda retreats.

sections.heading: July to September — Monsoon Magic
sections.body: India's monsoon is a spectacle in itself. Waterfalls surge across Goa, Meghalaya, and Western Ghats. Wildlife parks reopen with fresh vegetation and newborn animals. The crowds thin dramatically and prices drop — making this a great time for the seasoned traveller who doesn't mind the rain. Rajasthan and Ladakh remain mostly dry and perfect.

sections.heading: Our Recommendation
sections.body: For first-time visitors, October to February offers the best all-round experience. For adventure travellers, May to September opens extraordinary routes. For those seeking spiritual India — Varanasi is magical in any season, but the Ganga Aarti at dawn in winter is truly unforgettable.

sections.heading: Festivals — The Best Reason to Plan Around
sections.body: India's festival calendar is unlike anywhere else on earth. Diwali in October or November lights up every city with oil lamps and fireworks. Holi in March turns the streets of Mathura and Vrindavan into an explosion of colour. The Pushkar Camel Fair in Rajasthan draws thousands of traders, pilgrims, and travellers each November. If you can align your trip with even one major festival, do it — India in celebration is India at its most alive.

sections.heading: Visa & Entry — What to Know Before You Go
sections.body: Most nationalities can apply for an Indian e-Visa online, which covers tourism stays of up to 90 days. Processing typically takes 72 hours, but applying 2 weeks in advance is strongly recommended during peak season. The e-Visa is valid for two entries and is available for travellers from over 170 countries. Your Kesari Inbound trip planner can guide you through the exact requirements for your nationality.

articles.title: Rajasthan in 7 Days — Forts, Palaces & Desert Sunsets
articles.excerpt: Rajasthan is India at its most dramatic — a land of amber forts, royal palaces, and vast Thar Desert. Here is a perfect 7-day itinerary for first-time visitors.

sections.heading: Days 1–2: Jaipur — The Pink City
sections.body: Begin in Jaipur, Rajasthan's capital and one of India's most visually striking cities. Visit the Amber Fort at sunrise before the crowds arrive — the reflection of the fort in Maota Lake at dawn is extraordinary. Spend the afternoon at the City Palace and the iconic Hawa Mahal. Jaipur's bazaars, filled with block-printed textiles and blue pottery, deserve a full evening.

sections.heading: Days 3–4: Jodhpur & Ranakpur — The Blue City
sections.body: Drive to Jodhpur, where the old city is painted a vivid indigo blue — a sight visible for miles across the desert. Mehrangarh Fort looms above it all, one of India's most imposing fortresses. En route, stop at the stunning Jain temples of Ranakpur, carved entirely from white marble with over 1,400 pillars, each one unique.

sections.heading: Days 5–6: Jaisalmer — The Golden City
sections.body: Jaisalmer rises from the Thar Desert like a mirage — a golden sandstone fort city that has changed little in centuries. Explore the narrow lanes of the living fort, then head into the dunes for a sunset camel safari and an overnight camp under the stars. The silence of the desert at night is something you carry home forever.

sections.heading: Day 7: Udaipur — The City of Lakes
sections.body: End your journey in Udaipur, often described as the most romantic city in India. The City Palace rising over Lake Pichola is breathtaking at any hour. Take a boat ride at sunset, dine at a rooftop restaurant overlooking the water, and let Rajasthan's magic settle in fully before you depart.

sections.heading: Food, Shopping & Hidden Gems
sections.body: Rajasthani cuisine is bold and deeply satisfying — dal baati churma, laal maas, and ghewar are unmissable. In Jaipur, the street food around Johari Bazaar rivals any restaurant. For shopping, look for hand-block printed fabrics in Sanganer, silver jewellery in Jodhpur, and hand-woven carpets in Jaisalmer. Beyond the famous sights, ask your guide about village visits, puppet shows, and folk music evenings under the desert sky.

sections.heading: Practical Tips for Rajasthan
sections.body: Rajasthan is best visited between October and March. The state is large — distances between cities can be 4 to 6 hours by road, so factor travel time into your plan. Hiring a private car with a driver is the most comfortable and flexible option. Dress modestly when visiting temples and forts, carry a scarf, and stay hydrated — even in winter, the desert sun is strong by midday.

articles.title: Kerala — God's Own Country Complete Travel Guide
articles.excerpt: Kerala is India's most serene corner — a state of backwaters, spice plantations, Ayurveda retreats, and ancient temple festivals. Here is your complete guide.

sections.heading: The Backwaters — Kerala's Most Iconic Experience
sections.body: The backwaters of Alleppey (Alappuzha) are a network of lakes, canals, and lagoons stretching along Kerala's coast. The best way to experience them is aboard a traditional kettuvallam — a rice barge converted into a houseboat with bedrooms, a kitchen, and an open deck. Glide through villages where life moves at the pace of the water, passing fishermen, coconut groves, and temples half-hidden by lotus ponds.

sections.heading: Munnar — Tea Gardens in the Clouds
sections.body: Drive up into the Western Ghats to Munnar, where rolling hills are blanketed in manicured tea estates at an altitude of over 1,600 metres. The air is cool and fragrant, the mornings misty, and the sunsets over the green valleys deeply calming. Visit a working tea factory, trek to Eravikulam National Park to see the endangered Nilgiri Tahr, and simply breathe.

sections.heading: Ayurveda — Healing in Its Birthplace
sections.body: Kerala is the home of Ayurveda — the 5,000-year-old science of life. Treatments here are far more authentic and medically rigorous than elsewhere in India. A minimum 7-day Panchakarma detox programme at a certified retreat can address everything from chronic stress to joint pain. For a first-time experience, a 3-day rejuvenation package in Mararikulam or Kovalam is the perfect introduction.

sections.heading: Beaches, Spices & Wildlife
sections.body: Kerala's coast offers quieter, more cultured beach towns than Goa — Varkala's cliff-top cafés, Kovalam's lighthouse beach, and the untouched shores of Bekal in the north. Inland, Wayanad's spice plantations produce pepper, cardamom, and coffee amid dense jungle. Periyar Tiger Reserve offers boat safaris through a flooded forest where elephants come to drink at dawn.

sections.heading: Kathakali & Kerala's Arts Tradition
sections.body: Kerala has one of the richest performing arts traditions in India. Kathakali — the classical dance drama with its extraordinary costumes and elaborate face paint — is a complete theatrical world unto itself. Kalaripayattu, the ancient martial art that influenced kung fu, is still practised at dawn in village gymnasiums (kalaris) across the state. An evening spent watching a Kathakali performance, preceded by the two-hour make-up ceremony, is an experience that stays with you for life.

sections.heading: Getting Around & Best Time to Visit
sections.body: Kerala is well connected by road, rail, and air, with three international airports at Thiruvananthapuram, Kochi, and Calicut. The state is best visited between October and March, when the weather is dry and pleasant. The monsoon (June to September), while heavy on the coast, is actually the ideal season for Ayurveda — the humidity opens the pores and maximises the efficacy of treatments. A complete Kerala circuit — backwaters, hill stations, beaches, and wildlife — needs a minimum of 8 days.

articles.title: Spiritual India — Varanasi, Rishikesh & the Sacred Ghats
articles.excerpt: India's spiritual heartland is unlike anywhere else on earth. From the burning ghats of Varanasi to the ashrams of Rishikesh, this is a journey into the soul of an ancient civilisation.

sections.heading: Varanasi — The Eternal City
sections.body: Varanasi is one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities on earth, and the holiest city in Hinduism. Rise before dawn and take a wooden boat along the Ganges to watch the ghats slowly emerge from the mist — pilgrims bathing, priests performing rituals, smoke rising from the cremation pyres. It is raw, ancient, and profoundly moving. The evening Ganga Aarti, a ceremony of fire and devotion performed at the main ghat as darkness falls, is one of the most extraordinary spectacles in Asia.

sections.heading: Rishikesh — Where Yoga Meets the Himalaya
sections.body: Set where the Ganges emerges from the Himalayas, Rishikesh is the world capital of yoga. The Beatles famously came here in 1968 to study with Maharishi Mahesh Yogi, and the city has been a magnet for spiritual seekers ever since. Spend mornings in meditation or pranayama classes, afternoons by the river or white-water rafting, and evenings at the ashram for satsang. The Ram Jhula bridge, lit up at night with the sounds of temple bells, is magical.

sections.heading: Bodh Gaya & the Buddhist Circuit
sections.body: Bodh Gaya is where Siddhartha Gautama attained enlightenment under the Bodhi Tree — the most sacred site in Buddhism. The Mahabodhi Temple complex is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and a place of remarkable peace. Combine it with Sarnath (where the Buddha gave his first sermon) and Kushinagar (where he passed away) to complete the Buddhist pilgrimage circuit. Monks from Tibet, Thailand, Japan, and Sri Lanka all gather here in quiet devotion.

sections.heading: Planning Your Spiritual Journey
sections.body: The best time for spiritual India is October to March, when the weather is cool and the major festivals — Diwali, Kumbh Mela, and Holi — take place. The Kumbh Mela, held every 12 years, is the largest human gathering on earth. Kesari Inbound offers specially curated spiritual itineraries combining these destinations with authentic cultural immersion.

sections.heading: Amritsar & the Golden Temple
sections.body: No spiritual journey through India is complete without Amritsar, home to the Harmandir Sahib — the Golden Temple. The holiest shrine in Sikhism floats on the sacred Amrit Sarovar lake, its gilded dome reflecting in the still water. The langar (community kitchen) at the temple feeds over 100,000 people every single day, entirely free, run by volunteers. Arrive for the early morning ceremony when the holy scripture is carried in procession to the temple under a velvet canopy — a moment of profound, quiet devotion.

sections.heading: Mindful Travel — What to Expect and Respect
sections.body: Spiritual India demands a certain openness and humility. Remove footwear before entering any temple or mosque. Dress modestly — cover shoulders and knees. Photography is usually permitted in courtyards but not inside sanctuaries — always check. Engage with priests and guides genuinely, and resist the urge to rush from site to site. The spiritual depth of India reveals itself slowly, to those who are willing to sit still and simply observe.

articles.title: India Wildlife Safari — The Complete Tiger Reserve Guide
articles.excerpt: India is home to the world's largest population of wild tigers. With over 50 tiger reserves across the country, a wildlife safari here is a world-class experience.

sections.heading: Ranthambore — Rajasthan's Royal Tigers
sections.body: Ranthambore in Rajasthan is the most famous of India's tiger reserves — and with good reason. Tigers here have grown accustomed to safari vehicles and are frequently spotted in broad daylight, often lazing beside ancient ruins of the 10th-century Ranthambore Fort. The combination of wildlife and crumbling Mughal architecture is uniquely dramatic. October to April is the best time to visit, with February and March offering the highest tiger sighting rates.

sections.heading: Bandhavgarh — India's Highest Tiger Density
sections.body: Bandhavgarh in Madhya Pradesh has the highest density of Bengal tigers of any reserve in India. The park is compact, making safari drives efficient and sightings frequent. Beyond tigers, Bandhavgarh is home to leopards, sloth bears, gaur (Indian bison), and over 250 bird species. Stay at one of the boutique forest lodges on the edge of the park for an immersive experience from dawn till dusk.

sections.heading: Kaziranga — Rhinos and Elephants of Assam
sections.body: Kaziranga in Assam is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and home to two-thirds of the world's one-horned rhinoceros population. Elephant-back safaris at dawn through the tall grasslands — with rhinos, wild buffalo, and the occasional tiger emerging through the mist — are among the most extraordinary wildlife experiences in Asia. The park is at its best from November to April before the monsoon floods arrive.

sections.heading: Planning Your Wildlife Safari
sections.body: Most tiger reserves are open from October to June, with March to May offering the best sightings as animals gather around water sources in the heat. Safari vehicles are strictly regulated, with limited jeeps per zone per day — early booking is essential. Kesari Inbound's wildlife itineraries combine two or three reserves for maximum variety, with comfortable lodge stays and expert naturalist guides.

sections.heading: Jim Corbett & the Terai Forests
sections.body: India's oldest national park, Jim Corbett in Uttarakhand, sits at the foothills of the Himalayas where the Sal forests meet the Ramganga River. It is home to over 200 tigers and one of the finest birding destinations in South Asia with nearly 600 recorded species. The Dhikala forest zone — accessible only by jeep or elephant — offers multi-day stays at the forest rest house, putting you deep inside the reserve at dawn and dusk when wildlife movement is at its peak.

sections.heading: The Responsible Safari — Wildlife Travel Done Right
sections.body: India takes wildlife conservation seriously. All reserves operate under strict protocols — no off-roading, no feeding animals, no noise. The Forest Department limits daily visitor numbers in every zone to minimise stress on wildlife. Choose lodges with genuine conservation commitments: those that employ local naturalists, contribute to anti-poaching patrols, and use solar energy. A responsible safari not only gives you a better wildlife experience — it directly supports the protection of these extraordinary ecosystems for generations to come.
